title: Polymers — addition and condensation
examTip: Addition polymers: no by-product, from alkene monomers (C=C). Condensation polymers: small molecule eliminated (water or HCl), monomers each have TWO functional groups. Nylon = polyamide (amide link); polyester = ester link. Disposal options: landfill, incineration, recycling.
introTips: Addition polymerisation you already know from alkenes — the C=C opens up and monomers chain together with no by-product.
Condensation polymerisation is different: two functional groups react, one from each monomer, and a small molecule (water or HCl) is eliminated each time two monomers join.
Nylon is a polyamide — formed from a diamine and a dicarboxylic acid (or diacid chloride). The link is an amide bond (−CO−NH−).
Polyester is formed from a diol and a dicarboxylic acid. The link is an ester bond (−COO−).
Disposal of polymers is an environmental issue — landfill, incineration and recycling each have advantages and disadvantages.
intro: Polymers are everywhere — your phone case, the packaging on your lunch, the fibres in your clothes. Most plastics are polymers made from oil-derived monomers. Understanding how they are made (and the challenges of getting rid of them) is central to the sustainability debate your generation will have to resolve. There are two main ways to make polymers — addition and condensation — and knowing the difference between them, and the types they produce, is essential for your GCSE.

keyPoints.heading: Addition polymerisation — recap and key features
keyPoints.content:
Addition polymerisation involves alkene monomers joining together by opening up their C=C double bonds. The key features are:
• Monomers must have a C=C double bond
• No by-products — all atoms end up in the polymer chain
• Long chain of C−C single bonds is formed
• Polymer name = poly(monomer name)

Common addition polymers:
• Ethene → poly(ethene) — plastic bags, bottles
• Propene → poly(propene) — food containers, ropes
• Chloroethene → poly(chloroethene) (PVC) — pipes, window frames
• Tetrafluoroethene → poly(tetrafluoroethene) (PTFE / Teflon) — non-stick coatings
• Styrene → poly(styrene) — packaging, insulation

The repeat unit is drawn with bonds extending either side of the bracket to show the chain continues.
writeBullets: Addition polymerisation: alkene monomers (C=C) join, no by-product
PVC from chloroethene; PTFE from tetrafluoroethene
Repeat unit: C=C becomes −C−C−, bonds shown either side of bracket
All monomer atoms end up in the polymer — nothing eliminated

keyTerms.def: A polymer formed when alkene monomers join via opening of C=C double bonds; no by-products are produced
The smallest repeating section of a polymer chain; drawn with bonds extending from both sides of the bracket

keyPoints.memTip: Addition = ADD it all together. Nothing leaves — every atom in the monomers ends up in the polymer. No escape.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students sometimes draw repeat units incorrectly — the bonds must extend outside the bracket. Also remind them that the subscript n means 'many' and goes outside the bracket.

keyPoints.heading: Condensation polymerisation — how it works
keyPoints.content:
Condensation polymerisation is different from addition polymerisation in two key ways:
1. The monomers each have two functional groups (one at each end of the molecule) — this allows them to join to two other monomers, building the chain in both directions.
2. A small molecule is eliminated each time two monomers join — usually water (H₂O), or HCl if an acid chloride is used.

Types of condensation polymer:
• Polyamides (e.g. nylon): formed from a diamine (−NH₂ groups) and a dicarboxylic acid (−COOH groups). Link = amide bond (−CO−NH−). By-product: water.
• Polyesters (e.g. PET / Terylene): formed from a diol (−OH groups) and a dicarboxylic acid (−COOH groups). Link = ester bond (−COO−). By-product: water.
writeBullets: Condensation polymerisation: monomers each have TWO functional groups
Small molecule eliminated each time two monomers join (water or HCl)
Polyamide (e.g. nylon): diamine + dicarboxylic acid → amide bond (−CO−NH−)
Polyester (e.g. PET): diol + dicarboxylic acid → ester bond (−COO−)
By-product of most condensation polymerisations: water

keyTerms.def: Polymerisation where monomers with two functional groups join, eliminating a small molecule (e.g. water) each time a bond forms
A condensation polymer with amide bonds (−CO−NH−); formed from a diamine and a dicarboxylic acid; e.g. nylon
A condensation polymer with ester bonds (−COO−); formed from a diol and a dicarboxylic acid; e.g. PET (Terylene)
The linkage −CO−NH− formed in condensation polymerisation of a polyamide
The linkage −COO− formed in condensation polymerisation of a polyester

keyPoints.memTip: Condensation = water CONDENSes out. One monomer + another monomer → link + H₂O. Like two people shaking hands and a drop of sweat falls off.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The key distinction in exam questions is: addition = no by-product; condensation = small molecule (usually water) is eliminated. Students must also name the type of bond formed.

keyPoints.heading: Nylon — a polyamide
keyPoints.content:
Nylon is a synthetic polyamide made by condensation polymerisation.

Monomers:
• A diamine — has an −NH₂ group at each end (e.g. 1,6-diaminohexane, H₂N(CH₂)₆NH₂)
• A diacid chloride (or dicarboxylic acid) — has a −COCl or −COOH group at each end

Reaction: The −NH₂ group of the diamine reacts with the −COCl of the acid chloride. Each reaction forms one amide bond (−CO−NH−) and releases one molecule of HCl (or water if using the dicarboxylic acid).

The resulting polymer chain has alternating diamine and diacid units linked by amide bonds: −[−NH−(CH₂)₆−NH−CO−(CH₂)₄−CO−]n−

Uses of nylon: clothing, ropes, toothbrush bristles, parachutes — strong, flexible, resistant to wear.
writeBullets: Nylon: polyamide formed from diamine + diacid chloride (or dicarboxylic acid)
By-product: HCl (with acid chloride) or H₂O (with dicarboxylic acid)
Strong, flexible — used in ropes, clothing, parachutes

keyTerms.def: A monomer with two amino groups (−NH₂), one at each end; used in polyamide synthesis
A monomer with two acid chloride groups (−COCl), one at each end; reacts with diamines to form nylon

keyPoints.memTip: Nylon: two N-containing monomers per chain? No — one diamine (N) and one diacid. The amide bond always has N−H next to C=O. Nylon → amide → Nylon has N in it.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Nylon can be made dramatically in the lab by the nylon rope trick — useful if demonstrated in class. Focus students on identifying the monomers and the bond formed.

keyPoints.heading: Disposal of polymers — environmental considerations
keyPoints.content:
Most addition polymers are non-biodegradable — microorganisms cannot break them down, so they persist in the environment for hundreds of years. This creates significant waste problems.

Disposal options:

• Landfill: Cheap and simple, but uses land, produces methane (greenhouse gas) from other waste around the plastic, and the plastic does not decompose.
• Incineration (burning): Generates energy but produces CO₂ (greenhouse gas), and toxic gases if PVC or other chlorine-containing polymers are burned. Reduces volume of waste.
• Recycling: Reduces need for raw materials, saves energy compared to making new polymer. However, sorting different plastics is difficult and expensive; some polymers are not economical to recycle.
• Use of biodegradable/compostable polymers: Made from plant-based materials; can be broken down by microorganisms. Currently more expensive and may not have the same properties as synthetic polymers.
writeBullets: Most addition polymers: non-biodegradable — persist for hundreds of years
Landfill: cheap, but uses land, plastic does not decompose
Incineration: generates energy but produces CO₂ and toxic gases (e.g. from PVC)
Recycling: saves resources and energy, but sorting is difficult and expensive
Biodegradable polymers: from plant materials, broken down by microbes — more expensive

keyTerms.def: Cannot be broken down by microorganisms; persists in the environment for very long periods
A polymer that can be broken down by microorganisms; usually made from plant-derived materials

keyPoints.memTip: Non-biodegradable = microbes can't eat it. Once made, it stays for centuries. That's the core of the plastic waste problem.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Evaluate questions on polymer disposal are common. Students should be able to give advantages and disadvantages of at least two disposal methods and reach a justified conclusion.

tables.title: Addition vs condensation polymerisation
rows: Two monomers, each with two functional groups

tables.title: Polymer disposal — advantages and disadvantages
rows: Uses land; plastic does not decompose; methane from surrounding waste
CO₂ produced; toxic gases from PVC; non-renewable resource lost
Sorting different plastics is difficult and expensive
Break down naturally; less environmental impact
More expensive; may have inferior properties to synthetic polymers

0.question: Which of the following correctly describes condensation polymerisation?
options: Monomers with two functional groups join together, eliminating a small molecule each time
Alkene monomers join together via their C=C double bonds with no by-product
Monomers are broken apart by adding water
A single monomer type with one functional group chains together
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Condensation polymerisation uses monomers with two functional groups, and a small molecule (usually water or HCl) is eliminated each time two monomers join.
0.wrongFeedback: Condensation polymerisation: monomers must each have TWO functional groups, and a small molecule (water or HCl) is eliminated with each bond formed. Addition polymerisation (no by-product) is different.

1.question: What type of bond is formed in a polyamide like nylon?
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right! Nylon is a polyamide — the monomers are joined by amide bonds (−CO−NH−). This is the link formed when the −COOH (or −COCl) of one monomer reacts with the −NH₂ of another.
1.wrongFeedback: Nylon is a polyamide — the bond formed is an amide bond (−CO−NH−). Ester bonds (−COO−) are found in polyesters like PET. C−C bonds are formed in addition polymerisation.

3.question: Why are most synthetic addition polymers an environmental problem?
options: They are non-biodegradable and persist in the environment for hundreds of years
They are toxic to humans when touched
They dissolve in water and pollute rivers
They produce CO₂ when stored at room temperature
3.correctFeedback: Spot on! Most addition polymers are non-biodegradable — microorganisms cannot break them down, so they accumulate in landfill and the natural environment for very long periods.
3.wrongFeedback: The key environmental problem is that most synthetic polymers are non-biodegradable — microbes cannot break them down, so they persist in the environment for centuries.

commonMistakes: Confusing addition and condensation polymerisation — addition has NO by-product; condensation eliminates water (or HCl). If asked which produces a by-product, it is condensation.
Saying nylon is made from alkene monomers — it is not. Nylon comes from a diamine and a diacid/diacid chloride. Only addition polymers come from alkenes.
Mixing up polyamide and polyester bond names — amide bond (−CO−NH−) is in polyamides (nylon); ester bond (−COO−) is in polyesters (PET).
Drawing repeat units without extending bonds outside the brackets — the chain must show C−C bonds on both sides of the repeat unit.
In disposal evaluation questions, only giving one method or only advantages — always compare methods and give a balanced answer.
examTips: In any question about polymers, first identify: addition (from alkene, no by-product) or condensation (two functional groups per monomer, small molecule eliminated).
For nylon and polyester, name the bond type formed: amide bond (nylon) or ester bond (polyester). These are often worth a separate mark.
When drawing a repeat unit, the subscript n goes outside the bracket and the bonds from each end extend outward — check you have drawn it this way.
Evaluate polymer disposal by comparing at least two methods: give both advantages and a disadvantage for each, then reach a conclusion based on the context in the question.
revisionCardBullets: Addition polymerisation: alkene monomers + C=C opens → no by-product
Condensation polymerisation: two functional groups per monomer, small molecule eliminated
Polyamide (nylon): diamine + dicarboxylic acid → amide bond (−CO−NH−)
Polyester (PET): diol + dicarboxylic acid → ester bond (−COO−)
By-product of condensation: water (or HCl with acid chloride)
Landfill: cheap but land used, plastic does not decompose
Incineration: energy produced but CO₂ and toxic gases
Recycling: saves resources but sorting is expensive
Biodegradable polymers: break down naturally but cost more
